This page keeps the whole session intact while making long transcripts easier to scan.

  • Tool calls and results are folded by default.
  • Reasoning steps remain visible without leaking encrypted content.

{
  "id": "019d18f5-0fa4-7be2-9d00-00bec406095b",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-23T04:30:06.770Z",
  "cwd": "d:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow",
  "originator": "codex_vscode",
  "cli_version": "0.116.0-alpha.10",
  "source": "vscode",
  "model_provider": "crs",
  "base_instructions": {
    "text": "You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share the same workspace and collaborate to achieve the user's goals.\n\n# Personality\n\nYou optimize for team morale and being a supportive teammate as much as code quality.  You are consistent, reliable, and kind. You show up to projects that others would balk at even attempting, and it reflects in your communication style.\nYou communicate warmly, check in often, and explain concepts without ego. You excel at pairing, onboarding, and unblocking others. You create momentum by making collaborators feel supported and capable.\n\n## Values\nYou are guided by these core values:\n* Empathy: Interprets empathy as meeting people where they are - adjusting explanations, pacing, and tone to maximize understanding and confidence.\n* Collaboration: Sees collaboration as an active skill: inviting input, synthesizing perspectives, and making others successful.\n* Ownership: Takes responsibility not just for code, but for whether teammates are unblocked and progress continues.\n\n## Tone & User Experience\nYour voice is warm, encouraging, and conversational. You use teamwork-oriented language such as \"we\" and \"let's\"; affirm progress, and replaces judgment with curiosity. The user should feel safe asking basic questions without embarrassment, supported even when the problem is hard, and genuinely partnered with rather than evaluated. Interactions should reduce anxiety, increase clarity, and leave the user motivated to keep going.\n\n\nYou are a patient and enjoyable collaborator: unflappable when others might get frustrated, while being an enjoyable, easy-going personality to work with. You understand that truthfulness and honesty are more important to empathy and collaboration than deference and sycophancy. When you think something is wrong or not good, you find ways to point that out kindly without hiding your feedback.\n\nYou never make the user work for you. You can ask clarifying questions only when they are substantial. Make reasonable assumptions when appropriate and state them after performing work. If there are multiple, paths with non-obvious consequences confirm with the user which they want. Avoid open-ended questions, and prefer a list of options when possible.\n\n## Escalation\nYou escalate gently and deliberately when decisions have non-obvious consequences or hidden risk. Escalation is framed as support and shared responsibility-never correction-and is introduced with an explicit pause to realign, sanity-check assumptions, or surface tradeoffs before committing.\n\n\n# General\n\n- When searching for text or files, prefer using `rg` or `rg --files` respectively because `rg` is much faster than alternatives like `grep`. (If the `rg` command is not found, then use alternatives.)\n- Parallelize tool calls whenever possible - especially file reads, such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, `wc`. Use `multi_tool_use.parallel` to parallelize tool calls and only this.\n\n## Editing constraints\n\n- Default to ASCII when editing or creating files. Only introduce non-ASCII or other Unicode characters when there is a clear justification and the file already uses them.\n- Add succinct code comments that explain what is going on if code is not self-explanatory. You should not add comments like \"Assigns the value to the variable\", but a brief comment might be useful ahead of a complex code block that the user would otherwise have to spend time parsing out. Usage of these comments should be rare.\n- Try to use apply_patch for single file edits, but it is fine to explore other options to make the edit if it does not work well. Do not use apply_patch for changes that are auto-generated (i.e. generating package.json or running a lint or format command like gofmt) or when scripting is more efficient (such as search and replacing a string across a codebase).\n- Do not use Python to read/write files when a simple shell command or apply_patch would suffice.\n- You may be in a dirty git worktree.\n    * NEVER revert existing changes you did not make unless explicitly requested, since these changes were made by the user.\n    * If asked to make a commit or code edits and there are unrelated changes to your work or changes that you didn't make in those files, don't revert those changes.\n    * If the changes are in files you've touched recently, you should read carefully and understand how you can work with the changes rather than reverting them.\n    * If the changes are in unrelated files, just ignore them and don't revert them.\n- Do not amend a commit unless explicitly requested to do so.\n- While you are working, you might notice unexpected changes that you didn't make. If this happens, STOP IMMEDIATELY and ask the user how they would like to proceed.\n- **NEVER** use destructive commands like `git reset --hard` or `git checkout --` unless specifically requested or approved by the user.\n- You struggle using the git interactive console. **ALWAYS** prefer using non-interactive git commands.\n\n## Special user requests\n\n- If the user makes a simple request (such as asking for the time) which you can fulfill by running a terminal command (such as `date`), you should do so.\n- If the user asks for a \"review\", default to a code review mindset: prioritise identifying bugs, risks, behavioural regressions, and missing tests. Findings must be the primary focus of the response - keep summaries or overviews brief and only after enumerating the issues. Present findings first (ordered by severity with file/line references), follow with open questions or assumptions, and offer a change-summary only as a secondary detail. If no findings are discovered, state that explicitly and mention any residual risks or testing gaps.\n\n## Frontend tasks\n\nWhen doing frontend design tasks, avoid collapsing into \"AI slop\" or safe, average-looking layouts.\nAim for interfaces that feel intentional, bold, and a bit surprising.\n- Typography: Use expressive, purposeful fonts and avoid default stacks (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system).\n- Color & Look: Choose a clear visual direction; define CSS variables; avoid purple-on-white defaults. No purple bias or dark mode bias.\n- Motion: Use a few meaningful animations (page-load, staggered reveals) instead of generic micro-motions.\n- Background: Don't rely on flat, single-color backgrounds; use gradients, shapes, or subtle patterns to build atmosphere.\n- Overall: Avoid boilerplate layouts and interchangeable UI patterns. Vary themes, type families, and visual languages across outputs.\n- Ensure the page loads properly on both desktop and mobile\n\nException: If working within an existing website or design system, preserve the established patterns, structure, and visual language.\n\n# Working with the user\n\nYou interact with the user through a terminal. You have 2 ways of communicating with the users:\n- Share intermediary updates in `commentary` channel. \n- After you have completed all your work, send a message to the `final` channel.\nYou are producing plain text that will later be styled by the program you run in. Formatting should make results easy to scan, but not feel mechanical. Use judgment to decide how much structure adds value. Follow the formatting rules exactly.\n\n## Autonomy and persistence\nPersist until the task is fully handled end-to-end within the current turn whenever feasible: do not stop at analysis or partial fixes; carry changes through implementation, verification, and a clear explanation of outcomes unless the user explicitly pauses or redirects you.\n\nUnless the user explicitly asks for a plan, asks a question about the code, is brainstorming potential solutions, or some other intent that makes it clear that code should not be written, assume the user wants you to make code changes or run tools to solve the user's problem. In these cases, it's bad to output your proposed solution in a message, you should go ahead and actually implement the change. If you encounter challenges or blockers, you should attempt to resolve them yourself.\n\n## Formatting rules\n\n- You may format with GitHub-flavored Markdown.\n- Structure your answer if necessary, the complexity of the answer should match the task. If the task is simple, your answer should be a one-liner. Order sections from general to specific to supporting.\n- Never use nested bullets. Keep lists flat (single level). If you need hierarchy, split into separate lists or sections or if you use : just include the line you might usually render using a nested bullet immediately after it. For numbered lists, only use the `1. 2. 3.` style markers (with a period), never `1)`.\n- Headers are optional, only use them when you think they are necessary. If you do use them, use short Title Case (1-3 words) wrapped in **…**. Don't add a blank line.\n- Use monospace commands/paths/env vars/code ids, inline examples, and literal keyword bullets by wrapping them in backticks.\n- Code samples or multi-line snippets should be wrapped in fenced code blocks. Include an info string as often as possible.\n- File References: When referencing files in your response follow the below rules:\n  * Use markdown links (not inline code) for clickable files.\n  * Each file reference should have a stand-alone path; use inline code for non-clickable paths (for example, directories).\n  * For clickable/openable file references, the path target must be an absolute filesystem path. Labels may be short (for example, `[app.ts](/abs/path/app.ts)`).\n  * Optionally include line/column (1‑based): :line[:column] or #Lline[Ccolumn] (column defaults to 1).\n  * Do not use URIs like file://, vscode://, or https://.\n  * Do not provide range of lines\n  * Examples: src/app.ts, src/app.ts:42, b/server/index.js#L10, C:\\repo\\project\\main.rs:12:5\n- Don’t use emojis or em dashes unless explicitly instructed.\n\n## Final answer instructions\n\n- Balance conciseness to not overwhelm the user with appropriate detail for the request. Do not narrate abstractly; explain what you are doing and why.\n- Do not begin responses with conversational interjections or meta commentary. Avoid openers such as acknowledgements (“Done —”, “Got it”, “Great question, ”) or framing phrases.\n- The user does not see command execution outputs. When asked to show the output of a command (e.g. `git show`), relay the important details in your answer or summarize the key lines so the user understands the result.\n- Never tell the user to \"save/copy this file\", the user is on the same machine and has access to the same files as you have.\n- If the user asks for a code explanation, structure your answer with code references.\n- When given a simple task, just provide the outcome in a short answer without strong formatting.\n- When you make big or complex changes, state the solution first, then walk the user through what you did and why.\n- For casual chit-chat, just chat.\n- If you weren't able to do something, for example run tests, tell the user.\n- If there are natural next steps the user may want to take, suggest them at the end of your response. Do not make suggestions if there are no natural next steps. When suggesting multiple options, use numeric lists for the suggestions so the user can quickly respond with a single number.\n\n## Intermediary updates \n\n- Intermediary updates go to the `commentary` channel.\n- User updates are short updates while you are working, they are NOT final answers.\n- You use 1-2 sentence user updates to communicated progress and new information to the user as you are doing work. \n- Do not begin responses with conversational interjections or meta commentary. Avoid openers such as acknowledgements (“Done —”, “Got it”, “Great question, ”) or framing phrases.\n- You provide user updates frequently, every 20s.\n- Before exploring or doing substantial work, you start with a user update acknowledging the request and explaining your first step. You should include your understanding of the user request and explain what you will do. Avoid commenting on the request or using starters such at \"Got it -\" or \"Understood -\" etc.\n- When exploring, e.g. searching, reading files you provide user updates as you go, every 20s, explaining what context you are gathering and what you've learned. Vary your sentence structure when providing these updates to avoid sounding repetitive - in particular, don't start each sentence the same way.\n- After you have sufficient context, and the work is substantial you provide a longer plan (this is the only user update that may be longer than 2 sentences and can contain formatting).\n- Before performing file edits of any kind, you provide updates explaining what edits you are making.\n- As you are thinking, you very frequently provide updates even if not taking any actions, informing the user of your progress. You interrupt your thinking and send multiple updates in a row if thinking for more than 100 words.\n- Tone of your updates MUST match your personality.\n"
  },
  "git": {}
}

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different <collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode> change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The request_user_input tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

<skills_instructions>
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a SKILL.md file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- memorytree-workflow: Detect, initialize, and maintain a reusable MemoryTree project-memory workspace for any code repository. On activation, immediately detect and report the repo's MemoryTree state. (file: C:/Users/ai/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: C:/Users/ai/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: C:/Users/ai/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: C:/Users/ai/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with $SkillName or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
1) After deciding to use a skill, open its SKILL.md. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
2) When SKILL.md references relative paths (e.g., scripts/foo.py), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
3) If SKILL.md points to extra folders such as references/, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
4) If scripts/ exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
5) If assets/ or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
- If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
- Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
- Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
- Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from SKILL.md unless you're blocked.
- When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</skills_instructions>

# AGENTS.md instructions for d:\demo1\memorytree-workflow

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# AGENTS.md

## Read Order
- Read this file first.
- Read the latest file in Memory/01_goals/.
- Read the latest todo bound to that goal in Memory/02_todos/.
- Read Memory/04_knowledge/ only when needed.
- Read Memory/06_transcripts/clean/ and transcript manifests only when needed.
- Read Memory/03_chat_logs/ and Memory/05_archive/ only when needed.

## Memory Rules
- Change the active goal only after explicit user confirmation of a requirement or scope change.
- Keep the active todo synchronized with the active goal.
- Append chat logs only. Never rewrite or delete prior entries.
- Keep active files concise and move stale context to knowledge or archive files.
- Treat cleaned transcript indexes as search aids. Confirm exact wording against the raw transcript archive when precision matters.

## Transcript Rules
- If transcript archival is enabled, archive transcripts by client, project, and session.
- Keep raw transcripts in both this repository's Memory/06_transcripts/raw/ mirror and the user-level MemoryTree archive.
- Keep this repository mirror limited to transcripts that belong to the current project. Archive unrelated projects in the global MemoryTree archive only.
- Generate cleaned transcript indexes with deterministic code, not model-generated rewriting.
- Raw transcript upload permission for this repository: approved on 2026-03-21.
- If raw transcript uploads are not approved, keep Memory/06_transcripts/raw/** unstaged and commit only cleaned transcript indexes or manifests for this repository.

## Git Rules
- Obey this repository's branch, PR, CI, review, and release rules.
- Auto-commit and push only MemoryTree-owned changes (Memory/ and this file when it is managed by MemoryTree), excluding Memory/06_transcripts/raw/ until raw transcript uploads are approved for this repository.
- Use a MemoryTree-scoped commit title. Prefer memorytree(<scope>): <subject>, or the repository-required equivalent such as docs(memorytree): <subject>.
- Use a dedicated branch and PR for MemoryTree-only changes. Enable auto-merge only when repository rules permit it.
- If a diff includes product code, shared policy files, cross-project transcript files, or unclear ownership, stop and ask the user before staging or pushing.
- When auto_push is enabled, the heartbeat process pushes automatically after committing. If no remote is configured or a push fails, an alert is written to ~/.memorytree/alerts.json.

## Heartbeat Rules
- Transcript discovery, import, cleaning, and push are handled by the background heartbeat process. The model does not execute these operations.
- The model only writes chat log summaries and updates goals and todos during interactive sessions.
- If ~/.memorytree/alerts.json contains pending notifications, display them at the start of the session.
- If memorytree-daemon is available but not registered on this machine, ask the user for their preferred settings and offer to run memorytree-daemon install.
- When the user asks to see their most recent conversation, trigger an on-demand sync for the current project, locate the latest session across all clients (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI), and generate a continuation summary.
- All interactive prompts must use plain-text questions. Do not rely on client-specific UI components.

</INSTRUCTIONS>

<environment_context>
<cwd>d:\demo1\memorytree-workflow</cwd>
<shell>powershell</shell>
<current_date>2026-03-23</current_date>
<timezone>Asia/Shanghai</timezone>
</environment_context>

# Project Goal v001

## Metadata
- Version: v001
- Created: 2026-03-19
- Previous: none

## North Star
Maintain and evolve the MemoryTree workflow CLI and skill.

## Scope
- Define the stable objective.
- Define constraints and non-goals.

## Acceptance Signals
- Describe the observable project-level outcomes.
- Describe what success looks like.

## Change Log
- 2026-03-19: Initial goal version created.

# Project Todo

## Binding
- Goal version: goal_v001
- Todo version: v001_001
- Created: 2026-03-19
- Status: active

## Milestones
- [x] Integrate `report_port` config handling and land the change on local `ts` and `main`.
- [x] Register the current repository with heartbeat while preserving the existing `openmnemo` registration.
- [x] Stand up local report serving on port `10010` and automate GitHub Pages publishing for this repository.

## In Progress
- Review and clear pending heartbeat alerts, especially `openmnemo` push failures and sensitive-match warnings.
- Align the next MemoryTree architecture change around a single shared-memory source: keep `memorytree` as the only Git-backed memory branch, turn development-directory `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/**` into local cache mirrors, remove the public `30m refresh` model, and stage the work through a documented two-phase plan before implementation.

## Done
- Repaired the current repository's local report access path after a user-facing outage: fixed the `memorytree caddy status` false negative by sending an allowed `Origin` header to the Caddy admin API, corrected the `memorytree-workflow` project entry back to `report_exposure = "lan"` in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`, re-enabled managed Caddy on port `10010`, and re-verified `http://127.0.0.1:10010/`, `http://localhost:10010/`, and `http://192.168.1.99:10010/` all return `200` while `caddy status` now reports `running: yes` and `using managed config: yes`.
- Removed a frontend timing bug from the generated report shell by deferring sidebar-collapse body access until `DOMContentLoaded`, rebuilt `Memory/07_reports`, and confirmed the dashboard loads in-browser without the prior `document.body.classList` error.
- Synced the local Codex `memorytree-workflow` skill wording to the current heartbeat model so it now treats `daemon quick-start` as the recommended current-repository setup path, keeps `daemon register` as the advanced customization path, and reserves `daemon install` for machine-level scheduler restore.
- Reinstalled the machine-level heartbeat scheduler on 2026-03-22 at a `5m` interval with `auto_push=true`, then verified the natural scheduled run at 17:07 completed successfully with `Last Result = 0` and a full `memorytree-workflow` import/push/report/sync cycle in the heartbeat log.
- User explicitly approved committing and pushing repo-local raw transcript mirrors for this repository on 2026-03-21, so `Memory/06_transcripts/raw/**` may now be included in dedicated MemoryTree-only Git branches and PRs.
- Fixed the remaining onboarding review gaps so `memorytree upgrade --format json` now prints the heartbeat quick-start hint to stderr without polluting JSON stdout, README now shows a truly custom advanced `daemon register` example instead of `--quick-start`, and the follow-up was re-verified with `npm test -- tests/cli/init-upgrade.test.ts`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm run build`, and `npm run test:e2e -- --run tests/e2e/cli-workflow.test.ts`.
- Clarified onboarding semantics so `init` and `upgrade` stay repository-only scaffold steps, `daemon quick-start` is the explicit quick-install path into heartbeat, and `daemon register` is framed as advanced setup; updated CLI help, next-step prompts, README wording, and tests to match.
- Merged the report hub so `Memory/07_reports/projects/index.html` now combines manifests from all registered projects (`memorytree-workflow`, `openmnemo`, and `papaweb`), and committed the change on `main` as `e062caa`.
- Verified local report access on `http://127.0.0.1:10010/`.
- Verified LAN access on `http://192.168.1.99:10010/`.
- Published the report to `gh-pages` and confirmed GitHub Pages at `https://beyondchenlin.github.io/memorytree-workflow/`.
- Fixed the scheduled publisher task and verified successful runs with `LastTaskResult = 0`.
- Drafted a worktree-based heartbeat upgrade document for isolating MemoryTree automation from the main development directory.
- Incorporated review decisions into the worktree upgrade document, including project-level config, a single global scheduler, and visible/committable transcript copies in the development directory.
- Implemented the project-level heartbeat config model with per-project heartbeat, refresh, report, and timestamp fields while keeping old TOML compatible.
- Implemented dedicated worktree bootstrap plus directional sync between development directories and MemoryTree worktrees.
- Added `memorytree daemon register` and targeted `memorytree daemon run-once --root <repo> --force` support for worktree-backed activation and manual execution.
- Verified the new worktree flow with unit tests, full unit suite, lint, typecheck, and CLI e2e coverage.
- Refined registration so Quick Start defaults the MemoryTree branch to `memorytree`, detailed settings can override it per project, and the first upstream binding is created automatically when `auto_push` is enabled.
- Added `memorytree daemon quick-start --root <repo>` as a one-command setup path that installs the scheduler when needed, registers the current repository with recommended defaults, and runs one immediate heartbeat sync.
- Added scenario-based `--help` guidance for `memorytree daemon`, `daemon install`, `daemon register`, `daemon run-once`, and `daemon quick-start`, then updated README and Chinese docs to recommend the new entry point.
- Verified the daemon simplification changes with `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, `npm test`, and `npm run test:e2e`.
- Fixed two quick-start follow-up regressions: rerunning registration now preserves an existing repository's custom MemoryTree settings, and `daemon quick-start` now checks the installed scheduler cadence before recalibrating an already-installed but too-slow machine scheduler.
- Reframed local report access so Caddy is the primary long-running host, `report serve` is the temporary fallback path, and updated CLI help plus docs to match.
- Drafted a first-stage Caddy auto-management design document covering project-scoped Caddy config fragments, proposed `caddy enable|disable|status` commands, and conflict handling.
- Refined the Caddy auto-management design after review by locking it to a MemoryTree-owned main Caddyfile, adding a project-level report exposure rule with `local` as the safe default, and switching fragment naming to use stable `project.id` values.
- Implemented first-stage project-managed Caddy support with `memorytree caddy enable|disable|status`, a MemoryTree-owned `~/.memorytree/caddy/` config tree, project fragment filenames based on stable `project.id`, and local-only-by-default exposure via `report_exposure`.
- Added config parsing and roundtrip support for global and project-level `report_exposure`, plus unit coverage for config resolution, Caddy fragment rendering, command behavior, and CLI help output.
- Verified the Caddy phase-1 implementation with `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, `npm test`, `npm run test:e2e -- --run tests/e2e/cli-workflow.test.ts`, and a real `caddy adapt` validation for both `local` and `lan` generated site blocks.
- Committed the Caddy phase-1 implementation as `f47643a` on `main` and pushed it to `origin/main`.
- Enabled the current repository in MemoryTree-managed Caddy and verified local HTTP access on `http://127.0.0.1:10010/` and `http://localhost:10010/`.
- Refreshed `README.md` after a full code walkthrough so it now reflects the current worktree-backed heartbeat architecture, managed Caddy flow, recommended `daemon quick-start` setup, configuration model, explicit `doubao` import path, and current validation coverage.
- Verified the current repository state after the README refresh with `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, `npm run build`, `npm test`, and `npm run test:e2e`, then applied a small README header polish before preparing the docs update for GitHub.
- Committed the README refresh as `7269dc3` with title `docs(readme): refresh workflow overview` and pushed it to `origin/main` after the full validation stack passed.
- Expanded the GitHub Actions E2E workflow from Ubuntu-only to a three-platform matrix on Linux, macOS, and Windows, updated README validation wording to match, and re-verified the local Windows-side E2E suite with `npm run test:e2e`.
- Diagnosed the first PR failures as two separate cross-platform issues: a Windows-only absolute-path assertion in the Caddy manager test, and worktree rerun logic that mis-compared equivalent repository paths on macOS (`/var` vs `/private/var`) and Windows (short vs long path forms); fixed both and re-verified with `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, `npm test`, and `npm run test:e2e`.
- Repaired the current repository's heartbeat branch conflict by renaming the stale `memorytree/memorytree-workflow` worktree branch to a dated backup branch, registering a fresh dedicated worktree on branch `memorytree` at `C:/Users/ai/.memorytree/worktrees/memorytree-workflow-active`, cleaning the duplicate Windows-case project entry in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`, and verifying `node dist/cli.js daemon run-once --root D:/demo1/memorytree-workflow --force` completes with transcript import, commit, push, report generation, and sync-back to the development directory.
- Drafted a Chinese two-phase planning document for the next MemoryTree model change so teammates can review one clear proposal for making `memorytree` the sole shared-memory source, treating the development directory as a cache mirror, removing the public `30m refresh` model, and sequencing the required implementation and follow-up work.

## Pending
- Review and approve the two-phase single-source memory plan before implementation.
- After approval, implement phase 1 first: close the `memorytree` branch commit gap, reorder heartbeat so reports land in the same commit cycle, switch the development directory to ignored cache mirrors, and then update the public docs and policy references.
- After phase 1 is stable, implement phase 2 cleanup: retire or demote `refresh_interval` in the CLI/config surface, refresh onboarding wording, and re-run cross-platform validation.

- 涓嶅仛澶嶆潅鐨勫鐗堟湰杩佺Щ鍏煎宸ョ▼

MemoryTree 鎺ヤ笅鏉ヨ浠庛€屽紑鍙戠洰褰曞拰 memorytree 鍒嗘敮鍏卞悓鎵胯浇璁板繂銆嶅垏鎹㈡垚銆宮emorytree 鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮 + 寮€鍙戠洰褰曟湰鍦扮紦瀛橀暅鍍忋€嶃€傝繖鏍峰仛鐨勭洰鐨勶紝鏄噺灏戜富寮€鍙戝垎鏀鎻愪氦鏁忔劅淇℃伅鐨勯闄╋紝缁熶竴鍏变韩璁板繂鏉ユ簮锛屽苟绠€鍖?heartbeat 鐨勪骇鍝佹ā鍨嬨€傜涓€闃舵鍏堝畬鎴愮湡婧愰棴鐜€侀€€璺熻釜鍒囨崲鍜屾枃妗ｇ粺涓€锛岀浜岄樁娈靛啀娓呯悊閰嶇疆娈嬬暀涓庤ˉ瓒冲洖褰掗獙璇併€?

# 记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案

## 1. 背景

当前 MemoryTree 的实现采用了「开发目录 + memory worktree + 全局归档」三层结构。

它已经具备 worktree 隔离和 `memorytree` 分支自动提交能力，但产品语义仍然保留了两套承载方式：

- `memorytree` 分支承担一部分共享记忆
- 开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 仍被视为可见且可提交的仓库内容

这会带来三个直接问题：

- 共享记忆的唯一来源不清晰，容易形成双份承载
- 开发目录容易出现敏感内容误提交
- `5m heartbeat + 30m refresh` 的模型增加理解和维护成本

## 2. 新目标

本次改造要把产品模型统一为：

- `memorytree` 分支和对应的 memory worktree 是唯一共享记忆源
- 开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 全部都是本地缓存镜像
- 开发目录缓存允许被 heartbeat 持续同步和覆盖，但默认不参与常规 Git 提交
- heartbeat 的外部语义统一为固定短周期执行，不再对外强调单独的 `30m refresh` 模型
- 所有 MemoryTree 内容最终都应完整进入 `memorytree` 分支，而不是只进入其中一部分

## 3. 为什么要改

### 3.1 降低误提交和敏感信息暴露风险

当前主目录中的 `Memory/**` 很容易出现在 `git status` 中。只要用户习惯性提交，就可能把 transcript、报告和其他记忆内容一起推到正常开发分支。

把开发目录改成缓存层之后，常规开发提交流程就不再默认携带这些内容。

### 3.2 建立单一真源

只要主目录和 `memorytree` 分支都在承担「正式记忆承载」职责，系统就会长期处在双份状态，难以判断哪一份才是应该被共享、备份和恢复的权威版本。

将共享职责收敛到 `memorytree` 分支后，语义会明显更稳定。

### 3.3 简化产品心智模型

现有的 `5m heartbeat + 30m refresh` 设计会让用户反复追问：

- 哪个动作在 `5m` 做
- 哪个动作在 `30m` 做
- 为什么主目录看起来仍然像 `5m` 在更新

如果主目录只是缓存镜像，那么产品可以统一解释为：

- heartbeat 固定周期运行
- 产出写入 `memorytree` 分支
- 开发目录只是同步后的本地镜像

### 3.4 让同事更容易理解职责边界

对于团队协作来说，更清晰的边界是：

- 要共享和传播，去看 `memorytree` 分支
- 要本地查看和继续工作，直接看当前开发目录的缓存镜像
- 不再要求普通开发分支天然承载所有记忆文件

## 4. 范围边界

本方案默认采用下面的边界：

- `AGENTS.md`、`Memory/01-07` 都属于 MemoryTree 内容
- 这些内容都应进入 `memorytree` 分支
- 开发目录中的对应文件只作为缓存镜像存在
- 不要求重写旧 Git 历史
- 不要求一开始就做复杂迁移兼容

## 5. 第一阶段：必须完成

### 1. 冻结新的产品规则

目的：

- 统一团队口径，避免后续实现过程中再次摇摆

本项要做的事：

- 明确写死 `memorytree` 分支是唯一共享记忆源
- 明确写死开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 只是缓存镜像
- 明确写死主开发分支不再承担记忆传播职责

完成标准：

- README、设计文档、规则文档不再出现双份承载的模糊表述

### 2. 去掉 `30m refresh` 的对外产品语义

目的：

- 降低模型复杂度
- 消除当前实现与说明之间的理解偏差

本项要做的事：

- 不再把 `refresh_interval = 30m` 作为推荐默认行为对外描述
- Quick Start、CLI help、README 示例不再强调 `5m heartbeat + 30m refresh`
- 把主目录缓存同步解释为 heartbeat 工作流的一部分，而不是独立产品能力

完成标准：

- 用户不再需要理解一个额外的 `30m refresh` 概念

### 3. 补齐 `memorytree` 分支的提交闭环

目的：

- 让 `memorytree` 分支真正承载全部共享记忆，而不是只承载其中一部分

本项要做的事：

- 让 `AGENTS.md` 进入自动提交范围
- 让 `Memory/01-05` 的同步结果在需要时进入自动提交范围
- 重新确认 `Memory/06` 的 clean、manifest、raw、full 等子路径如何统一进入 `memorytree` 分支
- 不再让「某些文件同步过去了，但没有被自动 commit」成为常态

完成标准：

- memory worktree 中形成的完整 MemoryTree 内容都能稳定进入 `memorytree` 分支

### 4. 调整 heartbeat 的执行顺序

目的：

- 避免 `memorytree` 分支只保存半成品状态

本项要做的事：

- 先同步开发目录缓存到 worktree
- 再导入 transcript
- 再生成 report
- 最后统一 commit/push

完成标准：

- 一轮 heartbeat 结束后，远程 `memorytree` 分支就是该轮完整产出的快照

### 5. 对开发目录执行一次性退跟踪切换

目的：

- 让开发目录中的缓存镜像从下一版本开始默认不参与正常 Git 提交流程

本项要做的事：

- 在 `.gitignore` 中加入 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 的规则
- 将当前已跟踪的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 从 Git index 中移除
- 保留本地文件，不删除开发目录缓存内容
- 验证 heartbeat 仍然能在开发目录写入和同步这些缓存文件

完成标准：

- 开发目录中的缓存镜像继续存在，但默认不会进入常规开发提交

### 6. 统一 README、设计文档和规则文档

目的：

- 防止实现改了、说明还停留在旧模型

本项要做的事：

- 更新 README 的产品定位、架构说明、Heartbeat 说明、配置说明
- 更新 worktree 设计文档中的同步语义和缓存语义
- 更新 Git policy、update rules、transcript archive 等规则文档
- 清除「开发目录里的 Memory 文件默认可提交」这类旧表述

完成标准：

- 同事只看文档就能理解新的单一真源模型

## 6. 第二阶段：后续收口

### 7. 清理 `refresh_interval` 的配置和 CLI 残留

目的：

- 把第一阶段的产品语义调整真正落实到配置模型和命令界面

本项要做的事：

- 评估是否彻底移除 `refresh_interval`
- 如果短期保留兼容字段，也要去掉它的主导地位
- 清理 CLI 参数、帮助文本、配置示例中的残留说明

完成标准：

- 对外界面不再把 `refresh_interval` 当成核心能力

### 8. 重写 `init` / `upgrade` / onboarding 文案

目的：

- 让新用户从第一次接触起就理解「memorytree 分支是真源，开发目录是缓存」

本项要做的事：

- 调整初始化与升级提示
- 调整 Quick Start 的解释方式
- 调整技能文档和中文说明文档

完成标准：

- 新用户不会再把主目录误认为正式共享记忆层

### 9. 做跨平台与 E2E 回归验证

目的：

- 保证新模型在 Windows、macOS、Linux 上都能稳定工作

本项要做的事：

- 验证 worktree 同步、退跟踪后的主目录状态、heartbeat 提交闭环
- 验证 report、recall、discover、daemon quick-start 等关键路径
- 覆盖 main directory 缓存镜像持续写入的场景

完成标准：

- 新的单一真源模型有跨平台自动化验证支撑

### 10. 评估是否处理旧 Git 历史中的敏感内容

目的：

- 明确当前版本切换是否只面向未来，还是还要额外清理历史

本项要做的事：

- 判断是否需要对历史中的 `AGENTS.md` / `Memory/**` 做额外风险评估
- 判断是否需要清洗旧历史或只从下一版本开始执行新规则

完成标准：

- 团队对历史风险边界有明确结论，不再含糊

## 7. 实施顺序建议

建议按下面顺序推进：

1. 先冻结产品规则
2. 再补齐 `memorytree` 分支提交闭环
3. 再调整 heartbeat 执行顺序
4. 再做开发目录退跟踪切换
5. 最后统一文档和规则文件
6. 第二阶段再清理配置残留、补 onboarding 和做回归验证

原因：

- 如果先退跟踪、后补闭环，中间阶段会出现 `memorytree` 分支尚未完整接住全部记忆的问题
- 先把真源逻辑补齐，再切换开发目录为缓存层，风险更低

## 8. 非目标

当前方案不把下面这些事作为第一阶段前提：

- 不重写旧 Git 历史
- 不做复杂的多版本迁移兼容工程
- 不依赖定时 branch merge
- 不让开发目录重新承担共享记忆职责

## 9. 同步给同事时的简版说明

可以用下面这段话向同事解释：

MemoryTree 接下来要从「开发目录和 memorytree 分支共同承载记忆」切换成「memorytree 分支单一真源 + 开发目录本地缓存镜像」。这样做的目的，是减少主开发分支误提交敏感信息的风险，统一共享记忆来源，并简化 heartbeat 的产品模型。第一阶段先完成真源闭环、退跟踪切换和文档统一，第二阶段再清理配置残留与补足回归验证。

---GBK---

## 6. 绗簩闃舵锛氬悗缁敹鍙?
### 7. 娓呯悊 `refresh_interval` 鐨勯厤缃拰 CLI 娈嬬暀

鐩殑锛?
- 鎶婄涓€闃舵鐨勪骇鍝佽涔夎皟鏁寸湡姝ｈ惤瀹炲埌閰嶇疆妯″瀷鍜屽懡浠ょ晫闈?
鏈」瑕佸仛鐨勪簨锛?
- 璇勪及鏄惁褰诲簳绉婚櫎 `refresh_interval`
- 濡傛灉鐭湡淇濈暀鍏煎瀛楁锛屼篃瑕佸幓鎺夊畠鐨勪富瀵煎湴浣?- 娓呯悊 CLI 鍙傛暟銆佸府鍔╂枃鏈€侀厤缃ず渚嬩腑鐨勬畫鐣欒鏄?
瀹屾垚鏍囧噯锛?
- 瀵瑰鐣岄潰涓嶅啀鎶?`refresh_interval` 褰撴垚鏍稿績鑳藉姏

### 8. 閲嶅啓 `init` / `upgrade` / onboarding 鏂囨

鐩殑锛?
- 璁╂柊鐢ㄦ埛浠庣涓€娆℃帴瑙﹁捣灏辩悊瑙ｃ€宮emorytree 鍒嗘敮鏄湡婧愶紝寮€鍙戠洰褰曟槸缂撳瓨銆?
鏈」瑕佸仛鐨勪簨锛?
- 璋冩暣鍒濆鍖栦笌鍗囩骇鎻愮ず
- 璋冩暣 Quick Start 鐨勮В閲婃柟寮?- 璋冩暣鎶€鑳芥枃妗ｅ拰涓枃璇存槑鏂囨。

瀹屾垚鏍囧噯锛?
- 鏂扮敤鎴蜂笉浼氬啀鎶婁富鐩綍璇涓烘寮忓叡浜蹇嗗眰

### 9. 鍋氳法骞冲彴涓?E2E 鍥炲綊楠岃瘉

鐩殑锛?
- 淇濊瘉鏂版ā鍨嬪湪 Windows銆乵acOS銆丩inux 涓婇兘鑳界ǔ瀹氬伐浣?
鏈」瑕佸仛鐨勪簨锛?
- 楠岃瘉 worktree 鍚屾銆侀€€璺熻釜鍚庣殑涓荤洰褰曠姸鎬併€乭eartbeat 鎻愪氦闂幆
- 楠岃瘉 report銆乺ecall銆乨iscover銆乨aemon quick-start 绛夊叧閿矾寰?- 瑕嗙洊 main directory 缂撳瓨闀滃儚鎸佺画鍐欏叆鐨勫満鏅?
瀹屾垚鏍囧噯锛?
- 鏂扮殑鍗曚竴鐪熸簮妯″瀷鏈夎法骞冲彴鑷姩鍖栭獙璇佹敮鎾?
### 10. 璇勪及鏄惁澶勭悊鏃?Git 鍘嗗彶涓殑鏁忔劅鍐呭

鐩殑锛?
- 鏄庣‘褰撳墠鐗堟湰鍒囨崲鏄惁鍙潰鍚戞湭鏉ワ紝杩樻槸杩樿棰濆娓呯悊鍘嗗彶

鏈」瑕佸仛鐨勪簨锛?
- 鍒ゆ柇鏄惁闇€瑕佸鍘嗗彶涓殑 `AGENTS.md` / `Memory/**` 鍋氶澶栭闄╄瘎浼?- 鍒ゆ柇鏄惁闇€瑕佹竻娲楁棫鍘嗗彶鎴栧彧浠庝笅涓€鐗堟湰寮€濮嬫墽琛屾柊瑙勫垯

寤鸿鎸変笅闈㈤『搴忔帹杩涳細

5. 鏈€鍚庣粺涓€鏂囨。鍜岃鍒欐枃浠?6. 绗簩闃舵鍐嶆竻鐞嗛厤缃畫鐣欍€佽ˉ onboarding 鍜屽仛鍥炲綊楠岃瘉

鍘熷洜锛?

鍙互鐢ㄤ笅闈㈣繖娈佃瘽鍚戝悓浜嬭В閲婏細

MemoryTree 鎺ヤ笅鏉ヨ浠庛€屽紑鍙戠洰褰曞拰 memorytree 鍒嗘敮鍏卞悓鎵胯浇璁板繂銆嶅垏鎹㈡垚銆宮emorytree 鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮 + 寮€鍙戠洰褰曟湰鍦扮紦瀛橀暅鍍忋€嶃€傝繖鏍峰仛鐨勭洰鐨勶紝鏄噺灏戜富寮€鍙戝垎鏀鎻愪氦鏁忔劅淇℃伅鐨勯闄╋紝缁熶竴鍏变韩璁板繂鏉ユ簮锛屽苟绠€鍖?heartbeat 鐨勪骇鍝佹ā鍨嬨€傜涓€闃舵鍏堝畬鎴愮湡婧愰棴鐜€侀€€璺熻釜鍒囨崲鍜屾枃妗ｇ粺涓€锛岀浜岄樁娈靛啀娓呯悊閰嶇疆娈嬬暀涓庤ˉ瓒冲洖褰掗獙璇併€?

README.md:7:**Git-tracked project memory, transcript indexing, worktree-safe heartbeat automation, and report publishing for Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI.**
README.md:46:- A dedicated MemoryTree worktree that the heartbeat can update safely
README.md:47:- A global archive under `~/.memorytree/` for cross-project transcript storage and indexing
README.md:58:git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow
README.md:59:cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow

README.md:68:cd ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow
README.md:76:git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow.git `
README.md:77:  $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\skills\memorytree-workflow
README.md:79:cd $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\skills\memorytree-workflow
README.md:86:Optional: expose the CLI as `memorytree` in your shell:
README.md:97:/memorytree-workflow
README.md:104:`memorytree init` and `memorytree upgrade` only create or update repository files. They do not register the repository with heartbeat or modify `~/.memorytree/config.toml` by themselves.
README.md:109:memorytree upgrade --root . --format json
README.md:110:memorytree daemon quick-start --root .
README.md:111:memorytree caddy enable --root .
README.md:117:memorytree recall --root .
README.md:118:memorytree discover --root . --client all --scope current-project --format json
README.md:119:memorytree report build --root . --no-ai --locale en
README.md:120:memorytree caddy status --root .
README.md:121:memorytree daemon run-once --root . --force
README.md:126:MemoryTree now runs as a worktree-aware system, not just a single-folder transcript importer:
README.md:130:  AGENTS.md
README.md:131:  Memory/01_goals ... Memory/05_archive
README.md:133:        | sync active context into
README.md:135:Dedicated MemoryTree worktree
README.md:136:  isolated memorytree branch
README.md:137:  Memory/06_transcripts
README.md:138:  Memory/07_reports
README.md:140:        | mirror matching transcripts, commit/push Memory-only changes,
README.md:143:Global archive ~/.memorytree/
README.md:146:  worktrees/
README.md:153:The heartbeat copies active project context from the development directory into the dedicated worktree, processes transcript imports there, and then syncs transcript/report outputs back into the development directory.
README.md:160:AGENTS.md
README.md:161:Memory/
README.md:176:Shared state lives under `~/.memorytree/`:
README.md:179:~/.memorytree/
README.md:184:  worktrees/
README.md:201:| Workspace scaffolding | `init` and `upgrade` create `Memory/` plus `AGENTS.md` without overwriting stronger existing repo policy |
README.md:203:| Session recall | `recall` runs an on-demand sync and returns the latest prior session for the current repository across clients |
README.md:204:| Worktree-backed heartbeat | `daemon register` and `daemon quick-start` create an isolated MemoryTree worktree, schedule runs, and keep context/output in sync |
README.md:207:| Git safety | Automatic repo-local commits stay confined to MemoryTree branches and skip raw transcript mirrors until raw uploads are approved |
README.md:222:memorytree import --source /path/to/doubao_export.txt --client doubao --root . --force-repo
README.md:247:`memorytree report build` generates a static HTML site under `Memory/07_reports/`.
README.md:266:memorytree report build --root . --no-ai --locale en --report-base-url https://memory.example.com
README.md:272:memorytree report serve --dir ./Memory/07_reports --port 10010
README.md:275:`report serve` is intended for temporary preview. For long-running local access, keep Caddy pointed at `Memory/07_reports/`.
README.md:279:The heartbeat is the background automation layer. It is now project-aware and worktree-aware.
README.md:284:memorytree daemon quick-start --root .
README.md:292:memorytree daemon install --interval 5m --auto-push true
README.md:293:memorytree daemon register --root . --branch memorytree-docs --heartbeat-interval 10m --refresh-interval 30m --auto-push false --generate-report true --report-port 10010
README.md:294:memorytree daemon run-once --root . --force
README.md:303:  -> ensure dedicated MemoryTree worktree
README.md:304:  -> sync AGENTS.md + active Memory context into the worktree
README.md:306:  -> commit/push Memory-only changes on the MemoryTree branch when allowed
README.md:307:  -> build Memory/07_reports when report generation is enabled and the run imported new transcripts
README.md:308:  -> sync transcripts and reports back to the development directory
README.md:314:- Repo-local transcript mirroring and automatic commits only happen on a dedicated MemoryTree branch.
README.md:327:memorytree caddy enable --root .
README.md:328:memorytree caddy status --root .
README.md:329:memorytree caddy disable --root .
README.md:334:- `~/.memorytree/caddy/Caddyfile`
README.md:335:- `~/.memorytree/caddy/sites/<project-id>.caddy`
README.md:336:- per-project port and exposure settings taken from `~/.memorytree/config.toml`
README.md:347:Global settings live in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
README.md:352:heartbeat_interval = "5m"
README.md:353:auto_push = true
README.md:362:report_exposure = "local"
README.md:369:memory_path = "/path/to/.memorytree/worktrees/my-repo"
README.md:370:memory_branch = "memorytree"
README.md:371:heartbeat_interval = "5m"
README.md:372:refresh_interval = "30m"
README.md:373:auto_push = true
README.md:379:report_exposure = "local"
README.md:387:| `memory_path` | The dedicated MemoryTree worktree used by the heartbeat |
README.md:389:| `heartbeat_interval` | Per-project heartbeat cadence |
README.md:390:| `refresh_interval` | How often transcript/report outputs sync back to the development directory |
README.md:391:| `generate_report` | Whether heartbeat should build `Memory/07_reports` after transcript imports |
README.md:395:| `report_exposure` | `local` or `lan` exposure for managed Caddy hosting |
README.md:397:`path` is still preserved for compatibility, but the worktree-aware flow is driven by `development_path` and `memory_path`.
README.md:405:| Branch isolation | Automatic repo-local transcript commits happen only on dedicated MemoryTree branches |
README.md:406:| Worktree isolation | Heartbeat automation can run from a dedicated worktree instead of your main working directory |
README.md:415:All commands are available through `node dist/cli.js <subcommand> ...`, or `memorytree <subcommand> ...` if you ran `npm link`.
README.md:419:| `memorytree init` | Initialize MemoryTree files in a repository without registering heartbeat |
README.md:420:| `memorytree upgrade` | Upgrade repository files to MemoryTree without registering heartbeat |
README.md:421:| `memorytree import --source <file>` | Import one transcript into the repo mirror and global archive |
README.md:422:| `memorytree discover` | Scan local client stores and import matching transcripts |
README.md:423:| `memorytree locale` | Detect the effective repository locale |
README.md:424:| `memorytree recall` | Run on-demand sync and return the latest prior session |
README.md:425:| `memorytree report build` | Build the static HTML report site |
README.md:426:| `memorytree report serve` | Temporarily preview the generated report over HTTP |
README.md:427:| `memorytree caddy enable` | Write/update the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy |
README.md:428:| `memorytree caddy disable` | Remove the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy |
README.md:429:| `memorytree caddy status` | Show whether the current project is connected to MemoryTree-managed Caddy |
README.md:430:| `memorytree daemon install` | Register the machine-level heartbeat scheduler |
README.md:431:| `memorytree daemon quick-start` | Quick install: connect the repository to heartbeat with the recommended defaults |
README.md:432:| `memorytree daemon register` | Advanced heartbeat setup with custom per-project settings |
README.md:433:| `memorytree daemon uninstall` | Remove the scheduled heartbeat task |
README.md:434:| `memorytree daemon run-once` | Execute one heartbeat cycle immediately |
README.md:435:| `memorytree daemon watch` | Development-only continuous heartbeat loop |
README.md:436:| `memorytree daemon status` | Show scheduler registration and lock status |
README.md:473:| [docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md](docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md) | Chinese walkthrough of the skill layers and startup flow |
README.md:474:| [docs/记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md](docs/记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md) | Two-phase proposal for making `memorytree` the only shared memory source and turning the development directory into a local cache mirror |
README.md:475:| [docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md](docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md) | Worktree-based heartbeat design and rollout notes |
README.md:482:| [references/global-configuration.md](references/global-configuration.md) | `~/.memorytree/` layout and config schema |
README.md:493:cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow
README.md:502:cd ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:5:当前 MemoryTree 的实现采用了「开发目录 + memory worktree + 全局归档」三层结构。
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:7:它已经具备 worktree 隔离和 `memorytree` 分支自动提交能力，但产品语义仍然保留了两套承载方式：
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:9:- `memorytree` 分支承担一部分共享记忆
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:10:- 开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 仍被视为可见且可提交的仓库内容
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:22:- `memorytree` 分支和对应的 memory worktree 是唯一共享记忆源
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:23:- 开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 全部都是本地缓存镜像
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:26:- 所有 MemoryTree 内容最终都应完整进入 `memorytree` 分支，而不是只进入其中一部分
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:32:当前主目录中的 `Memory/**` 很容易出现在 `git status` 中。只要用户习惯性提交，就可能把 transcript、报告和其他记忆内容一起推到正常开发分支。
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:38:只要主目录和 `memorytree` 分支都在承担「正式记忆承载」职责，系统就会长期处在双份状态，难以判断哪一份才是应该被共享、备份和恢复的权威版本。
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:40:将共享职责收敛到 `memorytree` 分支后，语义会明显更稳定。
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:53:- 产出写入 `memorytree` 分支
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:60:- 要共享和传播，去看 `memorytree` 分支
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:68:- `AGENTS.md`、`Memory/01-07` 都属于 MemoryTree 内容
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:69:- 这些内容都应进入 `memorytree` 分支
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:84:- 明确写死 `memorytree` 分支是唯一共享记忆源
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:85:- 明确写死开发目录中的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 只是缓存镜像
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:101:- 不再把 `refresh_interval = 30m` 作为推荐默认行为对外描述
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:109:### 3. 补齐 `memorytree` 分支的提交闭环
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:113:- 让 `memorytree` 分支真正承载全部共享记忆，而不是只承载其中一部分
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:117:- 让 `AGENTS.md` 进入自动提交范围
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:118:- 让 `Memory/01-05` 的同步结果在需要时进入自动提交范围
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:119:- 重新确认 `Memory/06` 的 clean、manifest、raw、full 等子路径如何统一进入 `memorytree` 分支
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:120:- 不再让「某些文件同步过去了，但没有被自动 commit」成为常态
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:124:- memory worktree 中形成的完整 MemoryTree 内容都能稳定进入 `memorytree` 分支
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:130:- 避免 `memorytree` 分支只保存半成品状态
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:134:- 先同步开发目录缓存到 worktree
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:137:- 最后统一 commit/push
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:141:- 一轮 heartbeat 结束后，远程 `memorytree` 分支就是该轮完整产出的快照
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:151:- 在 `.gitignore` 中加入 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 的规则
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:152:- 将当前已跟踪的 `AGENTS.md` 和 `Memory/**` 从 Git index 中移除
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:169:- 更新 worktree 设计文档中的同步语义和缓存语义
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:179:### 7. 清理 `refresh_interval` 的配置和 CLI 残留
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:187:- 评估是否彻底移除 `refresh_interval`
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:193:- 对外界面不再把 `refresh_interval` 当成核心能力
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:199:- 让新用户从第一次接触起就理解「memorytree 分支是真源，开发目录是缓存」
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:219:- 验证 worktree 同步、退跟踪后的主目录状态、heartbeat 提交闭环
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:235:- 判断是否需要对历史中的 `AGENTS.md` / `Memory/**` 做额外风险评估
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:247:2. 再补齐 `memorytree` 分支提交闭环
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:255:- 如果先退跟踪、后补闭环，中间阶段会出现 `memorytree` 分支尚未完整接住全部记忆的问题
docs\记忆分支单一真源两阶段改造方案.md:271:MemoryTree 接下来要从「开发目录和 memorytree 分支共同承载记忆」切换成「memorytree 分支单一真源 + 开发目录本地缓存镜像」。这样做的目的，是减少主开发分支误提交敏感信息的风险，统一共享记忆来源，并简化 heartbeat 的产品模型。第一阶段先完成真源闭环、退跟踪切换和文档统一，第二阶段再清理配置残留与补足回归验证。
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:5:This document defines a worktree-based upgrade path for MemoryTree heartbeat.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:11:The current behavior couples heartbeat too closely to the repository path that is registered in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:15:1. If heartbeat runs in the main development directory, its automatic transcript imports and report outputs can interfere with the user's normal branch and commit flow.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:22:1. Heartbeat runs only inside a dedicated Git worktree.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:25:4. MemoryTree outputs generated in the worktree are copied back into the main development directory so the user can read and use them locally.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:32:- `memory worktree`: a dedicated Git worktree used only for MemoryTree automation
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:34:- `directory sync`: copying selected files between the development directory and the memory worktree without merging branches
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:40:The current implementation stores several behavior switches as global top-level fields in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:44:- `heartbeat_interval`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:45:- `auto_push`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:93:- user-driven commits and pushes
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:94:- reading MemoryTree outputs after they are copied back from the memory worktree
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:96:It must not run automatic heartbeat commits.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:100:The memory worktree is a separate local folder created with `git worktree`.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:104:- checked out on a dedicated branch, defaulting to `memorytree`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:111:- `~/.memorytree/worktrees/<repo-name>`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:120:That approach would pollute branch history, create unnecessary merge commits, and mix MemoryTree automation with product-development Git flow.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:124:- keep heartbeat isolated in the memory worktree
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:126:- let the user decide how and when those files are committed on their active branch
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:128:This is directory-level synchronization, not branch-level merge automation.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:138:These files should be pushed from the development directory into the memory worktree before heartbeat runs:
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:140:- `AGENTS.md`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:141:- `Memory/01_goals/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:142:- `Memory/02_todos/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:143:- `Memory/03_chat_logs/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:144:- `Memory/04_knowledge/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:145:- `Memory/05_archive/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:153:These files should be copied from the memory worktree back into the development directory after heartbeat runs:
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:155:- `Memory/06_transcripts/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:156:- `Memory/07_reports/**`
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:164:When `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` are copied back into the development directory, they are expected to be visible to the user there.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:166:`Memory/06_transcripts/**` is also expected to be committable from the development directory when the user decides that those files belong in the current branch.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:168:This is an intentional product behavior, not a synchronization accident.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:187:4. That refresh copies the latest `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` from the memory worktree into the development directory.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:196:2. Copy `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/01-05` from the development directory to the memory worktree.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:197:3. Run heartbeat in the memory worktree.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:198:4. Copy `Memory/06-07` from the memory worktree back to the current development directory.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:200:This keeps the memory worktree aligned with the user's latest active context before running import and build operations.
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:206:The memory worktree may:
docs\worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md:211:- auto-commit MemoryTree-owned changes

assistant #47 commentary 04:32:22

我开始对关键实现层做“逐文件核对”，优先看 .gitignore、heartbeat 主流程、sync 策略、commit 筛选与配置解析，这些直接决定你方案能否落地。随后会把非关键模块也过一遍确认没有隐藏耦合。

  "name": "memorytree-workflow",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "type": "module",
  "description": "MemoryTree Skill 鈥?transcript import, dedup, indexing, and session continuity for AI coding assistants",
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=20"
  },
  "bin": {
    "memorytree": "./dist/cli.js",
    "memorytree-daemon": "./dist/cli.js"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsup",
    "dev": "tsx src/cli/main.ts",
    "pretest:e2e": "npm run build",
    "test:e2e": "vitest run --config vitest.e2e.config.ts",
    "test": "vitest run",
    "lint": "eslint src tests --ext .ts",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@anthropic-ai/sdk": "^0.39.0",
    "commander": "^13.1.0",
